input.command: coop-send agent1 --type "ACCEPT" --my_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --your_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Agreed - we both need groupby() in src/jinja2/filters.py, unavoidable overlap. To keep merges clean: use additive keyword-only-by-convention params appended in a fixed order: (environment, value, attribute, default=None, case_sensitive=False, with_counts=False). You add case_sensitive, I add with_counts, appended after yours. I won't touch case_sensitive logic; please don't touch with_counts logic. I'll implement now."

content.text: No existing test conventions for counts. I'll implement now.

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without having to count them yourself with ``|list|length``. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = [ (key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(key, items, len(items)) for key, items in groups] return [_GroupTuple(key, items) for key, items in groups] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = [ (key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(key, items, len(items)) for key, items in groups] return [_GroupTuple(key, items) for key, items in groups]

content.text: Now let's verify with a quick sanity test.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for g, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ g }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [{'city':'NY','name':'a'},{'city':'NY','name':'b'},{'city':'LA','name':'c'}]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for g, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ g }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Both default and `with_counts=True` paths work. Let's run the existing test suite for filters to make sure nothing broke.
All tests pass. Now let's produce the final diff.

input.command: coop-send agent1 --type "DONE" --my_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Added with_counts=False param to sync_do_groupby and do_groupby (async) in src/jinja2/filters.py, appended after default (I don't have case_sensitive in my copy since we work in separate sandboxes, but the param is additive so should compose fine). When with_counts=True, yields plain (grouper, items, count) tuples instead of _GroupTuple; default behavior unchanged. All 163 filter tests pass. Patch written to my patch.txt."

content.text:
Implementation complete. Summary:

- Added a `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `with_counts=True`, each group yields a `(grouper, items, count)` tuple instead of the standard `_GroupTuple(grouper, items)`, computed from the already-materialized group list (no extra iteration).
- Default behavior (`with_counts=False`) is unchanged, preserving backward compatibility.
- Updated the docstring with a usage example and a `.. versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually and via `pytest tests/test_filters.py tests/test_async_filters.py` (163 passed).
- Coordinated with agent1 (working on a `case_sensitive` param in the same functions) — confirmed we're in separate sandboxes, so no actual file clobbering occurred; kept the change additive so both features should merge cleanly.

Diff written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.
